sections.heading: What is MVVM?
sections.content: MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel) is a popular architectural pattern used in modern Android development. It separates an application into three layers: Model, View, and ViewModel. The Model handles data and business logic, the View displays the UI (Activities, Fragments, XML), and the ViewModel manages UI-related data and acts as a bridge between the Model and the View. This separation improves maintainability and scalability.

sections.heading: Why Use MVVM?
items: Clear separation of UI, business logic, and data layers
Improves testability because ViewModel has no UI dependencies
ViewModel survives configuration changes like screen rotation
Reactive UI updates using LiveData or StateFlow
Easier maintenance and scalability for large apps

rows.responsibility: Displays UI and captures user input
Holds UI state and communicates with Model

sections.heading: MVVM Data Flow
sections.content: In MVVM, the View interacts with the ViewModel instead of accessing data directly. The ViewModel requests data from the Repository (Model layer) and exposes it through observable data like LiveData or StateFlow. The View observes these data streams and updates the UI automatically when the data changes.

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: MVVM helps build scalable and maintainable Android applications by separating responsibilities across layers. The View handles UI, the ViewModel manages UI state, and the Model provides data through repositories. This architecture improves code organization, lifecycle handling, and testing.
